daily_brief: The US-Israeli war against Iran entered its 18th day with significant developments on multiple fronts. Israel claimed to have killed Iran's security chief Ali Larijani and Basij commander Gholamreza Soleimani in overnight airstrikes [BBC, Globe and Mail, Le Monde], potentially the most significant targeting of Iranian leadership since Supreme Leader Khamenei was killed on February 28. In a notable break from the administration, National Counterterrorism Center Director Joe Kent resigned, stating Iran posed 'no imminent threat' and accusing Israeli lobbying of pushing the US into war [BBC, Le Monde, Globe and Mail]. President Trump's calls for NATO allies to help secure the Strait of Hormuz were rebuffed by European capitals, with Germany's defense minister asking what 'a handful of European frigates' could accomplish that the US Navy cannot [Le Monde, BBC]. The economic ripple effects are spreading globally: Sri Lanka announced a four-day work week to conserve fuel [ABC Australia], while British holidaymakers are rebooking Easter trips away from Dubai [BBC]. Separately, Pakistan conducted an airstrike on a Kabul drug rehabilitation center killing over 100 people [BBC], and a Brussels court ordered 93-year-old former Belgian diplomat Étienne Davignon to stand trial over the 1961 assassination of Congolese leader Patrice Lumumba [Guardian, RFI, France 24].

top_stories.headline: Israel Claims Killing of Iran's Security Chief Larijani in Escalating Air Campaign
bullets: Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz announced that Ali Larijani, secretary of Iran's Supreme National Security Council, and Basij paramilitary commander Gholamreza Soleimani were killed in overnight airstrikes near Tehran
Larijani had become the de facto leader of Iran's war efforts since Supreme Leader Khamenei was killed on February 28; his death would be the highest-profile assassination since then
Iran has not confirmed either death; both officials were central to Iran's violent crackdown on protests in January challenging the theocracy's rule
Prime Minister Netanyahu's office released a photo of him ordering the eliminations, while the IDF described Larijani as 'one of the most veteran and senior figures within the Iranian regime'

sources.name: BBC World
sources.framing_note: Detailed military and strategic context, emphasized Larijani's role as de facto leader

sources.name: Le Monde
sources.framing_note: Focused on Israeli government messaging and Netanyahu's involvement

sources.name: Globe and Mail
sources.framing_note: Emphasized broader war context including Gulf attacks and energy crisis concerns

sources.name: ABC Australia
sources.framing_note: Provided biographical background on Larijani as pragmatic diplomat

top_stories.headline: Top US Counterterrorism Official Resigns, Says Iran War Driven by Israeli Lobby Pressure
bullets: Joe Kent, director of the National Counterterrorism Center, resigned stating he 'cannot in good conscience support the ongoing war in Iran'
Kent's resignation letter accused the administration of starting the war 'due to pressure from Israel and its powerful American lobby,' claiming Iran posed 'no imminent threat'
Kent is a former Green Beret with 11 combat deployments; he is the first senior Trump administration official to resign over the Iran war
The White House dismissed the letter as containing 'many false claims'; Trump called Kent 'weak on security' and said the resignation showed 'it was a good thing that he's out'

sources.name: BBC World
sources.framing_note: Led with White House response and Trump's dismissive comments

sources.name: Le Monde
sources.framing_note: Emphasized Kent's military credentials and quoted extensively from resignation letter

sources.name: Globe and Mail
sources.framing_note: Noted Kent's past ties to right-wing extremists and controversial confirmation process

sources.name: The Hindu
sources.framing_note: Included background on Kent's previous congressional campaigns and far-right associations

top_stories.headline: European Allies Refuse Trump's Demand to Join Strait of Hormuz Operation
bullets: Trump called on NATO allies plus Japan, South Korea, and China to send warships to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, threatening it would be 'very bad for the future of NATO' if they refuse
EU foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas stated 'This is not Europe's war,' while Germany's defense minister asked what European frigates could do that the US Navy cannot
France confirmed it will not send naval vessels, maintaining a 'strictly defensive approach' and refusing to join the war on Iran
Trump later reversed course, posting that the US 'no longer need, or desire' allied assistance, adding 'WE DO NOT NEED THE HELP OF ANYONE'

sources.name: Le Monde
sources.framing_note: Detailed European diplomatic responses, emphasized France's refusal

sources.name: BBC Europe
sources.framing_note: Included criticism from former UK defense chief that NATO wasn't designed for 'wars of choice'

sources.name: The Hindu
sources.framing_note: Led with Trump's reversal claiming US doesn't need help

sources.name: RFI
sources.framing_note: Focused specifically on French position and defensive posture

top_stories.headline: Pakistan Airstrike Kills Over 100 at Kabul Drug Rehabilitation Center
bullets: Pakistani air strikes killed more than 100 people at the Omid Addiction Treatment Hospital in Kabul, according to forensic laboratory sources
The facility was treating approximately 2,000 patients; many bodies were reportedly injured beyond recognition
Pakistan denied deliberately targeting the hospital, claiming it struck 'military installations and terrorist support infrastructure'
The UN Assistance Mission in Afghanistan condemned the strike and called for an immediate ceasefire and investigation; Afghan health ministry said no military facilities were near the center

sources.name: BBC World
sources.framing_note: Detailed casualty figures and UN response, included Pakistan's denial

sources.name: France 24
sources.framing_note: Video coverage noting Pakistan's claim of targeting 'terrorist' targets

top_stories.headline: Former Belgian Diplomat Ordered to Stand Trial Over 1961 Assassination of Patrice Lumumba
bullets: Brussels court ordered 93-year-old Étienne Davignon, former EU Commission vice-president, to stand trial for alleged war crimes related to the killing of Congo's first prime minister
Davignon is charged with participation in unlawful detention and transfer of Lumumba and denying him a fair trial; a charge of intent to kill was dismissed
Lumumba was tortured and executed by firing squad in January 1961 by Belgian-backed separatists; his body was dissolved in acid
Lumumba's grandson Mehdi Lumumba called the decision 'historic,' saying 'Belgium is finally confronting its history'; Davignon denies all charges

sources.name: The Guardian
sources.framing_note: Emphasized historical context and Belgian parliamentary finding of 'moral responsibility'

sources.name: RFI
sources.framing_note: Framed as landmark moment in Europe's colonial reckoning

sources.name: France 24
sources.framing_note: Brief coverage noting this is first prosecution in the case
